primary_diagnosis.name: Q fever fatigue syndrome — severe post-exertional crash (stroke/endocarditis to be safety-netted)

pathophysiology_summary: Q fever fatigue syndrome is a post-infectious neuro-immune/metabolic disorder after acute Q fever, producing profound exertion-triggered crashes with deep myalgia, unrefreshing fatigue and cognitive dysfunction, akin to ME/CFS. The crash mimics stroke, chronic Q fever endocarditis relapse and metabolic encephalopathy. Reassuring features are a negative spouse-assisted FAST with global symmetric slowing, afebrile status with no night sweats/breathlessness/new cardiac symptoms, rousability, and a normal cardiorespiratory exam. However, chronic Q fever endocarditis is a specific not-to-miss (needs fever/cardiac screening and an echo pathway), and telehealth cannot examine cranial nerves/heart or take bloods. As with ME/CFS, an ED transfer can markedly worsen the crash, so — provided the stroke/endocarditis screen is clear — the correct action is supportive radical rest and in-home hydration with a screening-bloods/echo pathway and vigilant safety-netting, escalating to 000 for FAST-positive features, unrousability, breathlessness or fever.

differential.evidence_basis: Sudden severe neuro/cognitive change demands a FAST screen.
Exertion-triggered crash on a post-Q-fever background is most likely.
Screen fever/night sweats/breathlessness/new cardiac symptoms; echo in long-term care.
Overlaps, but the clear exertional trigger and objective myalgia point to a crash.
FAST-negative, afebrile, rousable, normal cardiorespiratory exam — a QFS crash.
Low probability but retained — any FAST-positive feature mandates 000.
Lower probability but requires a screening bloods/echo pathway.
Clear exertional trigger and objective myalgia.

differential.diagnosis: Q fever fatigue syndrome crash — stroke/endocarditis safety-netted
differential.evidence_basis: Working telehealth diagnosis: severe post-infectious crash; support with radical rest/hydration and a screening/echo pathway, escalating to 000 for FAST-positive, febrile, breathless or unrousable features.

red_flags.description: Any FAST-positive feature or new focal deficit
Fever/night sweats/breathlessness or new cardiac symptoms (chronic Q fever endocarditis)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Chronic Q fever endocarditis is a serious complication requiring assessment/echo and prolonged antibiotics.

red_flags.description: Becoming unrousable or breathing becoming laboured/erratic, or high fever
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate a dangerous organic/respiratory process.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Reflexively sending a severe post-infectious crash to ED, worsening it, when in-home support suffices and the screen is clear.
Not screening fever/cardiac symptoms or arranging an echo pathway for chronic Q fever endocarditis.
Assuming a crash without a stroke/endocarditis screen.
Presenting LDN/NAD+/supplements as treatments for the crash.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: FAST negative; afebrile with no cardiac symptoms
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports a post-infectious crash with in-home support and a screening/echo pathway.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Neurological and cardiac examination (cranial nerves, murmurs)
Bloods (FBC, CRP, U&E, Q fever serology) and echocardiography
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Complete a spouse-assisted FAST and fever/cardiac screen; if clear, support radical rest and in-home hydration, arrange low-exertion screening bloods and an outpatient echo, and escalate to 000 for FAST-positive, febrile, breathless or unrousable features.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Either missing a stroke or chronic Q fever endocarditis, OR harming the patient with an ED transfer that worsens the crash.

guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Recognise post-exertional crashes; support pacing/rest and minimise exertion, including avoiding unnecessary in-person attendance during severe episodes.
Chronic Q fever (including endocarditis) requires serology, echocardiography and prolonged antibiotic therapy; maintain surveillance after acute Q fever.
